question:
3. A projectile in flight is observed simultaneously from four radar stations which are situated at the corners of a square of side \( b \). The distances of the projectile from the four stations, taken in order around the square, are found to be \( \boldsymbol{R}_{1}, \boldsymbol{R}_{2}, \boldsymbol{R}_{3}, \boldsymbol{R}_{4} \). Show that
\[
R_{1}^{2}+R_{3}^{2}=R_{2}^{2}+R_{4}^{2}
\]

Show also that the height \( h \) of the projectile above the ground is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
h^{2}= & -\frac{1}{2} b^{2}+\frac{1}{4}\left(R_{1}^{2}+R_{2}^{2}+R_{3}^{2}+R_{4}^{2}\right) \\
& -\frac{1}{8 b^{2}}\left(R_{1}^{4}+R_{2}^{4}+R_{3}^{4}+R_{4}^{4}-2 R_{1}^{2} R_{3}^{2}-2 R_{2}^{2} R_{4}^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
solution:
Solution. Choose the diagonals of the square as axes in the plane. Then the four vertices are \( ( \pm a, 0),(0, \pm a) \), where \( a^{2}=b^{2} / 2 \), and we number them counterclockwise starting at \( (a, 0) \). If the projectile is above the point \( (x, y) \), then
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
R_{1}^{2}=(x-a)^{2}+y^{2}+h^{2}, & R_{2}^{2}=x^{2}+(y-a)^{2}+h^{2} \\
R_{3}^{2}=(x+a)^{2}+y^{2}+h^{2}, & R_{4}^{2}=x^{2}+(y+a)^{2}+h^{2}
\end{array}
\]

Hence, \( R_{1}{ }^{2}+R_{3}{ }^{2}=R_{2}{ }^{2}+R_{4}{ }^{2} \), as required, and
\[
R_{1}^{2}+R_{2}^{2}+R_{3}^{2}+R_{4}^{2}-4 a^{2}-4 h^{2}=4\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right)
\]

Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
R_{1}^{4}+R_{2}^{4} & +R_{3}^{4}+R_{4}^{4}-2 R_{1}^{2} R_{3}^{2}-2 R_{2}^{2} R_{4}^{2} \\
& =\left(R_{3}^{2}-R_{1}^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(R_{4}^{2}-R_{2}^{2}\right)^{2}=16 a^{2}\left(x^{2}+y^{2}\right) \\
& =4 a^{2}\left(R_{1}^{2}+R_{2}^{2}+R_{3}^{2}+R_{4}^{2}-4 a^{2}-4 h^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence,
\[
\begin{array}{c}
16 a^{2} h^{2}=-16 a^{4}+4 a^{2}\left(R_{1}^{2}+R_{2}^{2}+R_{3}^{2}+R_{4}^{2}\right) \\
-\left(R_{1}^{4}+R_{2}^{4}+R_{3}^{4}+R_{4}^{4}-2 R_{1}^{2} R_{3}^{2}-2 R_{2}^{2} R_{4}^{2}\right)
\end{array}
\]

Dividing through by \( 16 a^{2}=8 b^{2} \), we obtain the desired relation.
Remark 1. To keep the algebra simple, it is essential to take advantage of the symmetry of the square. Another, almost equally good, choice of coordinates would make the vertices \( ( \pm c, \pm c) \), where \( c=b / 2 \).

Remark 2. A point above a plane is determined by its distances from any three non-collinear points in the plane. Hence \( h, x \), and \( y \) are determined by any three of the \( R \) 's. This explains why there must be a nontrivial relation connecting the four R's.

descriptive_long.question:
3. A projectile in flight is observed simultaneously from four radar stations which are situated at the corners of a square of side \( sidesquare \). The distances of the projectile from the four stations, taken in order around the square, are found to be \( \boldsymbol{rangeone}, \boldsymbol{rangetwo}, \boldsymbol{rangethree}, \boldsymbol{rangefour} \). Show that
\[
rangeone^{2}+rangethree^{2}=rangetwo^{2}+rangefour^{2}
\]

Show also that the height \( heightp \) of the projectile above the ground is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
heightp^{2}= & -\frac{1}{2} sidesquare^{2}+\frac{1}{4}\left(rangeone^{2}+rangetwo^{2}+rangethree^{2}+rangefour^{2}\right) \\
& -\frac{1}{8 sidesquare^{2}}\left(rangeone^{4}+rangetwo^{4}+rangethree^{4}+rangefour^{4}-2 rangeone^{2} rangethree^{2}-2 rangetwo^{2} rangefour^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Choose the diagonals of the square as axes in the plane. Then the four vertices are \( ( \pm diaghalf, 0),(0, \pm diaghalf) \), where \( diaghalf^{2}=sidesquare^{2} / 2 \), and we number them counterclockwise starting at \( (diaghalf, 0) \). If the projectile is above the point \( (coordx, coordy) \), then
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
rangeone^{2}=(coordx-diaghalf)^{2}+coordy^{2}+heightp^{2}, & rangetwo^{2}=coordx^{2}+(coordy-diaghalf)^{2}+heightp^{2} \\
rangethree^{2}=(coordx+diaghalf)^{2}+coordy^{2}+heightp^{2}, & rangefour^{2}=coordx^{2}+(coordy+diaghalf)^{2}+heightp^{2}
\end{array}
\]

Hence, \( rangeone^{2}+rangethree^{2}=rangetwo^{2}+rangefour^{2} \), as required, and
\[
rangeone^{2}+rangetwo^{2}+rangethree^{2}+rangefour^{2}-4 diaghalf^{2}-4 heightp^{2}=4\left(coordx^{2}+coordy^{2}\right)
\]

Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
rangeone^{4}+rangetwo^{4} & +rangethree^{4}+rangefour^{4}-2 rangeone^{2} rangethree^{2}-2 rangetwo^{2} rangefour^{2} \\
& =\left(rangethree^{2}-rangeone^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(rangefour^{2}-rangetwo^{2}\right)^{2}=16 diaghalf^{2}\left(coordx^{2}+coordy^{2}\right) \\
& =4 diaghalf^{2}\left(rangeone^{2}+rangetwo^{2}+rangethree^{2}+rangefour^{2}-4 diaghalf^{2}-4 heightp^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence,
\[
\begin{array}{c}
16 diaghalf^{2} heightp^{2}=-16 diaghalf^{4}+4 diaghalf^{2}\left(rangeone^{2}+rangetwo^{2}+rangethree^{2}+rangefour^{2}\right) \\
-\left(rangeone^{4}+rangetwo^{4}+rangethree^{4}+rangefour^{4}-2 rangeone^{2} rangethree^{2}-2 rangetwo^{2} rangefour^{2}\right)
\end{array}
\]

Dividing through by \( 16 diaghalf^{2}=8 sidesquare^{2} \), we obtain the desired relation.
Remark 1. To keep the algebra simple, it is essential to take advantage of the symmetry of the square. Another, almost equally good, choice of coordinates would make the vertices \( ( \pm halfside, \pm halfside) \), where \( halfside=sidesquare / 2 \).

Remark 2. A point above a plane is determined by its distances from any three non-collinear points in the plane. Hence \( heightp, coordx \), and \( coordy \) are determined by any three of the \( R \) 's. This explains why there must be a nontrivial relation connecting the four R's.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
3. A projectile in flight is observed simultaneously from four radar stations which are situated at the corners of a square of side \( horseshoe \). The distances of the projectile from the four stations, taken in order around the square, are found to be \( \boldsymbol{earthquake}, \boldsymbol{sugarcane}, \boldsymbol{tidalwave}, \boldsymbol{blacksmith} \). Show that
\[
earthquake^{2}+tidalwave^{2}=sugarcane^{2}+blacksmith^{2}
\]

Show also that the height \( driftwood \) of the projectile above the ground is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
driftwood^{2}= & -\frac{1}{2} horseshoe^{2}+\frac{1}{4}\left(earthquake^{2}+sugarcane^{2}+tidalwave^{2}+blacksmith^{2}\right) \\
& -\frac{1}{8 horseshoe^{2}}\left(earthquake^{4}+sugarcane^{4}+tidalwave^{4}+blacksmith^{4}-2 earthquake^{2} tidalwave^{2}-2 sugarcane^{2} blacksmith^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Choose the diagonals of the square as axes in the plane. Then the four vertices are \( ( \pm starflower, 0),(0, \pm starflower) \), where \( starflower^{2}=horseshoe^{2} / 2 \), and we number them counterclockwise starting at \( (starflower, 0) \). If the projectile is above the point \( (almondtree, paintbrush) \), then
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
earthquake^{2}=(almondtree-starflower)^{2}+paintbrush^{2}+driftwood^{2}, & sugarcane^{2}=almondtree^{2}+(paintbrush-starflower)^{2}+driftwood^{2} \\
tidalwave^{2}=(almondtree+starflower)^{2}+paintbrush^{2}+driftwood^{2}, & blacksmith^{2}=almondtree^{2}+(paintbrush+starflower)^{2}+driftwood^{2}
\end{array}
\]

Hence, \( earthquake{ }^{2}+tidalwave{ }^{2}=sugarcane{ }^{2}+blacksmith{ }^{2} \), as required, and
\[
earthquake^{2}+sugarcane^{2}+tidalwave^{2}+blacksmith^{2}-4 starflower^{2}-4 driftwood^{2}=4\left(almondtree^{2}+paintbrush^{2}\right)
\]

Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
earthquake^{4}+sugarcane^{4} & +tidalwave^{4}+blacksmith^{4}-2 earthquake^{2} tidalwave^{2}-2 sugarcane^{2} blacksmith^{2} \\
& =\left(tidalwave^{2}-earthquake^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(blacksmith^{2}-sugarcane^{2}\right)^{2}=16 starflower^{2}\left(almondtree^{2}+paintbrush^{2}\right) \\
& =4 starflower^{2}\left(earthquake^{2}+sugarcane^{2}+tidalwave^{2}+blacksmith^{2}-4 starflower^{2}-4 driftwood^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence,
\[
\begin{array}{c}
16 starflower^{2} driftwood^{2}=-16 starflower^{4}+4 starflower^{2}\left(earthquake^{2}+sugarcane^{2}+tidalwave^{2}+blacksmith^{2}\right) \\
-\left(earthquake^{4}+sugarcane^{4}+tidalwave^{4}+blacksmith^{4}-2 earthquake^{2} tidalwave^{2}-2 sugarcane^{2} blacksmith^{2}\right)
\end{array}
\]

Dividing through by \( 16 starflower^{2}=8 horseshoe^{2} \), we obtain the desired relation.
Remark 1. To keep the algebra simple, it is essential to take advantage of the symmetry of the square. Another, almost equally good, choice of coordinates would make the vertices \( ( \pm brainstorm, \pm brainstorm) \), where \( brainstorm=horseshoe / 2 \).

Remark 2. A point above a plane is determined by its distances from any three non-collinear points in the plane. Hence \( driftwood, almondtree \), and \( paintbrush \) are determined by any three of the \( R \) 's. This explains why there must be a nontrivial relation connecting the four R's.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
3. A projectile in flight is observed simultaneously from four radar stations which are situated at the corners of a square of side \( narrowness \). The distances of the projectile from the four stations, taken in order around the square, are found to be \( \boldsymbol{closenessone}, \boldsymbol{closenesstwo}, \boldsymbol{closenessthree}, \boldsymbol{closenessfour} \). Show that
\[
closenessone^{2}+closenessthree^{2}=closenesstwo^{2}+closenessfour^{2}
\]

Show also that the height \( depthvalue \) of the projectile above the ground is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
depthvalue^{2}= & -\frac{1}{2} narrowness^{2}+\frac{1}{4}\left(closenessone^{2}+closenesstwo^{2}+closenessthree^{2}+closenessfour^{2}\right) \\
& -\frac{1}{8 narrowness^{2}}\left(closenessone^{4}+closenesstwo^{4}+closenessthree^{4}+closenessfour^{4}-2 closenessone^{2} closenessthree^{2}-2 closenesstwo^{2} closenessfour^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Choose the diagonals of the square as axes in the plane. Then the four vertices are \( ( \pm shortmeasure, 0),(0, \pm shortmeasure) \), where \( shortmeasure^{2}=narrowness^{2} / 2 \), and we number them counterclockwise starting at \( (shortmeasure, 0) \). If the projectile is above the point \( (verticalaxis, horizontalaxis) \), then
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
closenessone^{2}=(verticalaxis-shortmeasure)^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}+depthvalue^{2}, & closenesstwo^{2}=verticalaxis^{2}+(horizontalaxis-shortmeasure)^{2}+depthvalue^{2} \\
closenessthree^{2}=(verticalaxis+shortmeasure)^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}+depthvalue^{2}, & closenessfour^{2}=verticalaxis^{2}+(horizontalaxis+shortmeasure)^{2}+depthvalue^{2}
\end{array}
\]

Hence, \( closenessone^{2}+closenessthree^{2}=closenesstwo^{2}+closenessfour^{2} \), as required, and
\[
closenessone^{2}+closenesstwo^{2}+closenessthree^{2}+closenessfour^{2}-4 shortmeasure^{2}-4 depthvalue^{2}=4\left(verticalaxis^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}\right)
\]

Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
closenessone^{4}+closenesstwo^{4} & +closenessthree^{4}+closenessfour^{4}-2 closenessone^{2} closenessthree^{2}-2 closenesstwo^{2} closenessfour^{2} \\
& =\left(closenessthree^{2}-closenessone^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(closenessfour^{2}-closenesstwo^{2}\right)^{2}=16 shortmeasure^{2}\left(verticalaxis^{2}+horizontalaxis^{2}\right) \\
& =4 shortmeasure^{2}\left(closenessone^{2}+closenesstwo^{2}+closenessthree^{2}+closenessfour^{2}-4 shortmeasure^{2}-4 depthvalue^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence,
\[
\begin{array}{c}
16 shortmeasure^{2} depthvalue^{2}=-16 shortmeasure^{4}+4 shortmeasure^{2}\left(closenessone^{2}+closenesstwo^{2}+closenessthree^{2}+closenessfour^{2}\right) \\
-\left(closenessone^{4}+closenesstwo^{4}+closenessthree^{4}+closenessfour^{4}-2 closenessone^{2} closenessthree^{2}-2 closenesstwo^{2} closenessfour^{2}\right)
\end{array}
\]

Dividing through by \( 16 shortmeasure^{2}=8 narrowness^{2} \), we obtain the desired relation.
Remark 1. To keep the algebra simple, it is essential to take advantage of the symmetry of the square. Another, almost equally good, choice of coordinates would make the vertices \( ( \pm microextent, \pm microextent) \), where \( microextent=narrowness / 2 \).

Remark 2. A point above a plane is determined by its distances from any three non-collinear points in the plane. Hence \( depthvalue, verticalaxis \), and \( horizontalaxis \) are determined by any three of the closeness 's. This explains why there must be a nontrivial relation connecting the four closeness's.

garbled_string.question:
3. A projectile in flight is observed simultaneously from four radar stations which are situated at the corners of a square of side \( snzptcwa \). The distances of the projectile from the four stations, taken in order around the square, are found to be \( \boldsymbol{qzxwvtnp}, \boldsymbol{hjgrksla}, \boldsymbol{vmclqser}, \boldsymbol{dpnfrxug} \). Show that
\[
qzxwvtnp^{2}+vmclqser^{2}=hjgrksla^{2}+dpnfrxug^{2}
\]

Show also that the height \( jbvwlecm \) of the projectile above the ground is given by
\[
\begin{aligned}
jbvwlecm^{2}= & -\frac{1}{2} snzptcwa^{2}+\frac{1}{4}\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}+vmclqser^{2}+dpnfrxug^{2}\right) \\
& -\frac{1}{8 snzptcwa^{2}}\left(qzxwvtnp^{4}+hjgrksla^{4}+vmclqser^{4}+dpnfrxug^{4}-2 qzxwvtnp^{2} vmclqser^{2}-2 hjgrksla^{2} dpnfrxug^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Choose the diagonals of the square as axes in the plane. Then the four vertices are \( ( \pm fvyqkdje, 0),(0, \pm fvyqkdje) \), where \( fvyqkdje^{2}=snzptcwa^{2} / 2 \), and we number them counterclockwise starting at \( (fvyqkdje, 0) \). If the projectile is above the point \( (tmprqzvn, kplhxsqt) \), then
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
qzxwvtnp^{2}=(tmprqzvn-fvyqkdje)^{2}+kplhxsqt^{2}+jbvwlecm^{2}, & hjgrksla^{2}=tmprqzvn^{2}+(kplhxsqt-fvyqkdje)^{2}+jbvwlecm^{2} \\
vmclqser^{2}=(tmprqzvn+fvyqkdje)^{2}+kplhxsqt^{2}+jbvwlecm^{2}, & dpnfrxug^{2}=tmprqzvn^{2}+(kplhxsqt+fvyqkdje)^{2}+jbvwlecm^{2}
\end{array}
\]

Hence, \( qzxwvtnp^{2}+vmclqser^{2}=hjgrksla^{2}+dpnfrxug^{2} \), as required, and
\[
qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}+vmclqser^{2}+dpnfrxug^{2}-4 fvyqkdje^{2}-4 jbvwlecm^{2}=4\left(tmprqzvn^{2}+kplhxsqt^{2}\right)
\]

Therefore,
\[
\begin{aligned}
qzxwvtnp^{4}+hjgrksla^{4} & +vmclqser^{4}+dpnfrxug^{4}-2 qzxwvtnp^{2} vmclqser^{2}-2 hjgrksla^{2} dpnfrxug^{2} \\
& =\left(vmclqser^{2}-qzxwvtnp^{2}\right)^{2}+\left(dpnfrxug^{2}-hjgrksla^{2}\right)^{2}=16 fvyqkdje^{2}\left(tmprqzvn^{2}+kplhxsqt^{2}\right) \\
& =4 fvyqkdje^{2}\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}+vmclqser^{2}+dpnfrxug^{2}-4 fvyqkdje^{2}-4 jbvwlecm^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
\]

Hence,
\[
\begin{array}{c}
16 fvyqkdje^{2} jbvwlecm^{2}=-16 fvyqkdje^{4}+4 fvyqkdje^{2}\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+hjgrksla^{2}+vmclqser^{2}+dpnfrxug^{2}\right) \\
-\left(qzxwvtnp^{4}+hjgrksla^{4}+vmclqser^{4}+dpnfrxug^{4}-2 qzxwvtnp^{2} vmclqser^{2}-2 hjgrksla^{2} dpnfrxug^{2}\right)
\end{array}
\]

Dividing through by \( 16 fvyqkdje^{2}=8 snzptcwa^{2} \), we obtain the desired relation.

Remark 1. To keep the algebra simple, it is essential to take advantage of the symmetry of the square. Another, almost equally good, choice of coordinates would make the vertices \( ( \pm rslbpjmh, \pm rslbpjmh) \), where \( rslbpjmh=snzptcwa / 2 \).

Remark 2. A point above a plane is determined by its distances from any three non-collinear points in the plane. Hence \( jbvwlecm, tmprqzvn \), and \( kplhxsqt \) are determined by any three of the \( R \) 's. This explains why there must be a nontrivial relation connecting the four R's.

kernel_variant.question:
Twelve synchronized beacons are fixed at the vertices  
P_1 ,\ldots , P_{12} of a regular dodecagon which lies in the horizontal plane.  
Its side-length is d > 0; the centre O coincides with the origin.  
Number the vertices counter-clockwise, starting with the one on the positive x-axis, so that  

 P_k = a exp(i\theta _k) with \theta _k = (k - 1)\cdot \pi /6  (k = 1,\ldots ,12),

where the circum-radius a is linked to the side-length by  

 d = 2a sin(\pi /12)   (a = d /(2 sin 15^\circ) = \frac{1}{2}d(\sqrt{6}+\sqrt{2})).

At one instant an aircraft A = (x, y, h) with altitude h > 0 is observed.  
Put z = x + iy and denote the simultaneous ranges

 L_k = |A P_k|  (k = 1,\ldots ,12).

Introduce the power sums  

 S = \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} L_k^2,  
 Q = \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} L_k^4 - 2 \Sigma _{k=1}^{6} L_k^2 L_{k+6}^2  (indices mod 12).

(a) (Discrete Fourier annihilation)  
Let  

 \tilde L_r := \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} e^{2\pi  i r (k-1)/12}\,L_k^2  (r\in \mathbb{Z}).

Show that  

 \tilde L_r = 0 whenever r (mod 12) \notin  {0,\pm 1}.(1)

Equivalently, the only non-vanishing Fourier modes of the sequence {L_k^2} occur for the
frequencies r\equiv 0, 1, 11 (mod 12).
In particular

 L_1^2 - L_2^2 + L_3^2 - L_4^2 + \cdots  - L_{12}^2 = 0   (r = 6),  
 L_1^2+L_3^2+L_5^2+L_7^2+L_9^2+L_{11}^2 = L_2^2+L_4^2+L_6^2+L_8^2+L_{10}^2+L_{12}^2  (r = 4),  
 L_1^2 - L_3^2 + L_5^2 - L_7^2 + L_9^2 - L_{11}^2 = 0  (r = 2)  

together with three further independent alternating relations (r = 8, 10\equiv -2 and r = -4).

(b) (Altitude formula)  
Prove that the aircraft altitude is determined by the ranges through  

 h^2 = -a^2 + S/12 - Q /(48 a^2)  
    = -d^2 /(4 sin^2 15^\circ) + S/12 - Q sin^2 15^\circ /(12 d^2).

(c) (Horizontal position and a built-in consistency check)  
Define the first two non-trivial Fourier coefficients

 F_1 = \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} e^{2\pi  i (k-1)/12}\,L_k^2,  F_2 = \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} e^{4\pi  i (k-1)/12}\,L_k^2.

Show that  

 F_2 = 0  and  z = -F_1 /(12 a).(2)

Consequently  

 |z|^2 = Q /(48 a^2). (3)

Thus the triple (x, y, h) is uniquely determined by the twelve measured ranges,
while the single identity F_2 = 0 supplies an immediate experimental consistency
check for the measured data.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write \zeta  := e^{2\pi  i/12}; hence \zeta ^{12} = 1 and \zeta ^6 = -1.

1. Expressing the squared ranges.  
For every k

 L_k^2 = |z - a\zeta ^{k-1}|^2 + h^2  
    = |z|^2 + a^2 + h^2 - 2a Re( z \zeta ^{-(k-1)} ).

Introduce  

 \rho  := |z|^2 + a^2 + h^2, u_k := Re( z \zeta ^{-(k-1)} ),(4)

so that  

 L_k^2 = \rho  - 2a u_k.(5)

Because the twelve roots \zeta ^{k-1} are uniformly distributed, we shall repeatedly use  

 \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} \zeta ^{m(k-1)} = 0  if m\not\equiv 0 (mod 12).(6)

2. Proof of (a).  
Let r\in \mathbb{Z}.  Compute the discrete Fourier coefficient

 \tilde L_r = \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} \zeta ^{r(k-1)} L_k^2  
           = \rho  \Sigma  \zeta ^{r(k-1)} - 2a \Sigma  u_k \zeta ^{r(k-1)}.(7)

The first sum vanishes unless r\equiv 0 (mod 12).  
For the second one write u_k as half the sum of two conjugate exponentials,

 u_k = \frac{1}{2}( z \zeta ^{-(k-1)} + \bar z \zeta ^{k-1} ).

Hence

 \Sigma  u_k \zeta ^{r(k-1)}  
  = \frac{1}{2} z \Sigma  \zeta ^{(r-1)(k-1)} + \frac{1}{2} \bar z \Sigma  \zeta ^{(r+1)(k-1)}.(8)

Each geometric series on the right is non-zero only when r-1\equiv 0 or r+1\equiv 0 (mod 12), i.e. when r\equiv 1 or r\equiv 11.  Therefore

 \tilde L_r \neq  0 \Leftrightarrow  r \equiv  0, 1, 11 (mod 12), proving (1).(9)

Particular alternating identities.  
* r = 6 \to  \zeta ^{6(k-1)} = (-1)^{k-1} gives the first displayed relation.  
* r = 4 (or r = 8) separates odd from even indices, yielding the second.  
* r = 2 (or r = 10) produces the third one cited in the statement; altogether six independent real relations arise from r = 2,4,6,8,10,-2.

3. Global power sums S and Q.  
Summing (5) immediately gives

 S = \Sigma  L_k^2 = 12\rho . (10)

For fourth powers we square (5):

 L_k^4 = (\rho  - 2a u_k)^2 = \rho ^2 - 4a\rho  u_k + 4a^2u_k^2.

Because \Sigma  u_k = 0 (by (6) with m = 1),

 \Sigma  L_k^4 = 12\rho ^2 + 4a^2U, where U := \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} u_k^2.(11)

Opposite beacons are antipodal: P_{k+6} = -P_k, hence u_{k+6} = -u_k and from (5)

 L_{k+6}^2 = \rho  + 2a u_k.(12)

Therefore for k = 1,\ldots ,6

 L_k^2 L_{k+6}^2 = (\rho  - 2a u_k)(\rho  + 2a u_k) = \rho ^2 - 4a^2u_k^2,

and

 \Sigma _{k=1}^{6} L_k^2 L_{k+6}^2 = 6\rho ^2 - 2a^2U. (13)

Combining (11) and (13) yields the corrected coefficient

 Q = \Sigma  L_k^4 - 2 \Sigma _{k=1}^{6} L_k^2 L_{k+6}^2  
   = (12\rho ^2 + 4a^2U) - 2(6\rho ^2 - 2a^2U) = 8a^2U. (14)

4. Evaluation of U = \Sigma  u_k^2.  
Write z = |z| e^{i\varphi }.  Then u_k = |z| cos(\varphi  - \theta _k).  
Because the twelve directions \theta _k are equally spaced, the average of cos^2 over them is \frac{1}{2}, whence

 U = \Sigma  u_k^2 = 12\cdot |z|^2 / 2 = 6|z|^2.(15)

(One may also confirm this with roots of unity:  
U = \frac{1}{4}(z^2 \Sigma  \zeta ^{-2(k-1)} + \bar z^2 \Sigma  \zeta ^{2(k-1)} + 2|z|^2 \Sigma  1) = 6|z|^2.)

Substituting (15) into (14) gives

 Q = 48 a^2 |z|^2 \Leftrightarrow  |z|^2 = Q /(48 a^2). (16)

5. Altitude formula (part (b)).  
From (10) we have \rho  = S/12.  By definition \rho  = a^2 + h^2 + |z|^2, so

 h^2 = \rho  - a^2 - |z|^2 = S/12 - a^2 - Q /(48 a^2),(17)

which is exactly the asserted expression.

6. The first two Fourier coefficients (part (c)).  
First coefficient:

 F_1 = \Sigma  \zeta ^{k-1} L_k^2  
     = \Sigma  \zeta ^{k-1}(\rho  - 2a u_k)  
     = -2a \Sigma  u_k \zeta ^{k-1}  (because \Sigma  \zeta ^{k-1}=0).(18)

Using (8) with r = 1 we obtain

 \Sigma  u_k \zeta ^{k-1} = \frac{1}{2} z \Sigma  \zeta ^{0} + \frac{1}{2} \bar z \Sigma  \zeta ^{2(k-1)} = 6z,(19)

since \Sigma  \zeta ^{0}=12 and \Sigma  \zeta ^{2(k-1)}=0.  Hence

 F_1 = -12a z \Leftrightarrow  z = -F_1 /(12 a). (20)

Second coefficient:

 F_2 = \Sigma  \zeta ^{2(k-1)} L_k^2  
    = -2a \Sigma  u_k \zeta ^{2(k-1)}.(21)

Insert the decomposition of u_k again:

 \Sigma  u_k \zeta ^{2(k-1)}  
  = \frac{1}{2} z \Sigma  \zeta ^{(2-1)(k-1)} + \frac{1}{2} \bar z \Sigma  \zeta ^{(2+1)(k-1)}  
  = \frac{1}{2} z \Sigma  \zeta ^{(k-1)} + \frac{1}{2} \bar z \Sigma  \zeta ^{3(k-1)} = 0, (22)

because both geometric sums vanish by (6).  Therefore

 F_2 = 0. (23)

Equations (20) and (16) complete part (c).  The identity F_2 = 0 furnishes an
immediate consistency test: any experimental data with F_2\neq 0 cannot come from
a single point in space.

Thus all three coordinates (x, y, h) are uniquely reconstructed from the twelve
ranges, and the corrected derivation is internally consistent.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Higher dimension in the sense of a 12-vertex configuration introduces six independent complex linear identities instead of one (square) or two (hexagon).  
• The solution demands a discrete Fourier transform on the vertex set, not merely an alternating–sum trick.  Orthogonality of 12th roots of unity, manipulation of complex exponentials and symmetry arguments are indispensable.  
• Altitude extraction now requires handling both fourth powers ΣL_k⁴ and the cross terms ΣL_k²L_{k+6}², together with an evaluation of Σu_k² via averaging of trigonometric squares—several more algebraic steps than in the hexagon case.  
• Part (c) asks for the full horizontal coordinates, forcing the solver to connect Fourier coefficients with geometric data, something entirely absent from the original problem.  
Overall the problem intertwines discrete Fourier analysis, classical geometry of regular polygons, and symmetric-polynomial elimination, making it markedly more sophisticated and lengthier than both the original square problem and the existing hexagon variant.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Twelve synchronized beacons are fixed at the vertices  
P_1 ,\ldots , P_{12} of a regular dodecagon which lies in the horizontal plane.  
Its side-length is d > 0; the centre O coincides with the origin.  
Number the vertices counter-clockwise, starting with the one on the positive x-axis, so that  

 P_k = a exp(i\theta _k) with \theta _k = (k - 1)\cdot \pi /6  (k = 1,\ldots ,12),

where the circum-radius a is linked to the side-length by  

 d = 2a sin(\pi /12)   (a = d /(2 sin 15^\circ) = \frac{1}{2}d(\sqrt{6}+\sqrt{2})).

At one instant an aircraft A = (x, y, h) with altitude h > 0 is observed.  
Put z = x + iy and denote the simultaneous ranges

 L_k = |A P_k|  (k = 1,\ldots ,12).

Introduce the power sums  

 S = \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} L_k^2,  
 Q = \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} L_k^4 - 2 \Sigma _{k=1}^{6} L_k^2 L_{k+6}^2  (indices mod 12).

(a) (Discrete Fourier annihilation)  
Let  

 \tilde L_r := \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} e^{2\pi  i r (k-1)/12}\,L_k^2  (r\in \mathbb{Z}).

Show that  

 \tilde L_r = 0 whenever r (mod 12) \notin  {0,\pm 1}.(1)

Equivalently, the only non-vanishing Fourier modes of the sequence {L_k^2} occur for the
frequencies r\equiv 0, 1, 11 (mod 12).
In particular

 L_1^2 - L_2^2 + L_3^2 - L_4^2 + \cdots  - L_{12}^2 = 0   (r = 6),  
 L_1^2+L_3^2+L_5^2+L_7^2+L_9^2+L_{11}^2 = L_2^2+L_4^2+L_6^2+L_8^2+L_{10}^2+L_{12}^2  (r = 4),  
 L_1^2 - L_3^2 + L_5^2 - L_7^2 + L_9^2 - L_{11}^2 = 0  (r = 2)  

together with three further independent alternating relations (r = 8, 10\equiv -2 and r = -4).

(b) (Altitude formula)  
Prove that the aircraft altitude is determined by the ranges through  

 h^2 = -a^2 + S/12 - Q /(48 a^2)  
    = -d^2 /(4 sin^2 15^\circ) + S/12 - Q sin^2 15^\circ /(12 d^2).

(c) (Horizontal position and a built-in consistency check)  
Define the first two non-trivial Fourier coefficients

 F_1 = \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} e^{2\pi  i (k-1)/12}\,L_k^2,  F_2 = \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} e^{4\pi  i (k-1)/12}\,L_k^2.

Show that  

 F_2 = 0  and  z = -F_1 /(12 a).(2)

Consequently  

 |z|^2 = Q /(48 a^2). (3)

Thus the triple (x, y, h) is uniquely determined by the twelve measured ranges,
while the single identity F_2 = 0 supplies an immediate experimental consistency
check for the measured data.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write \zeta  := e^{2\pi  i/12}; hence \zeta ^{12} = 1 and \zeta ^6 = -1.

1. Expressing the squared ranges.  
For every k

 L_k^2 = |z - a\zeta ^{k-1}|^2 + h^2  
    = |z|^2 + a^2 + h^2 - 2a Re( z \zeta ^{-(k-1)} ).

Introduce  

 \rho  := |z|^2 + a^2 + h^2, u_k := Re( z \zeta ^{-(k-1)} ),(4)

so that  

 L_k^2 = \rho  - 2a u_k.(5)

Because the twelve roots \zeta ^{k-1} are uniformly distributed, we shall repeatedly use  

 \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} \zeta ^{m(k-1)} = 0  if m\not\equiv 0 (mod 12).(6)

2. Proof of (a).  
Let r\in \mathbb{Z}.  Compute the discrete Fourier coefficient

 \tilde L_r = \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} \zeta ^{r(k-1)} L_k^2  
           = \rho  \Sigma  \zeta ^{r(k-1)} - 2a \Sigma  u_k \zeta ^{r(k-1)}.(7)

The first sum vanishes unless r\equiv 0 (mod 12).  
For the second one write u_k as half the sum of two conjugate exponentials,

 u_k = \frac{1}{2}( z \zeta ^{-(k-1)} + \bar z \zeta ^{k-1} ).

Hence

 \Sigma  u_k \zeta ^{r(k-1)}  
  = \frac{1}{2} z \Sigma  \zeta ^{(r-1)(k-1)} + \frac{1}{2} \bar z \Sigma  \zeta ^{(r+1)(k-1)}.(8)

Each geometric series on the right is non-zero only when r-1\equiv 0 or r+1\equiv 0 (mod 12), i.e. when r\equiv 1 or r\equiv 11.  Therefore

 \tilde L_r \neq  0 \Leftrightarrow  r \equiv  0, 1, 11 (mod 12), proving (1).(9)

Particular alternating identities.  
* r = 6 \to  \zeta ^{6(k-1)} = (-1)^{k-1} gives the first displayed relation.  
* r = 4 (or r = 8) separates odd from even indices, yielding the second.  
* r = 2 (or r = 10) produces the third one cited in the statement; altogether six independent real relations arise from r = 2,4,6,8,10,-2.

3. Global power sums S and Q.  
Summing (5) immediately gives

 S = \Sigma  L_k^2 = 12\rho . (10)

For fourth powers we square (5):

 L_k^4 = (\rho  - 2a u_k)^2 = \rho ^2 - 4a\rho  u_k + 4a^2u_k^2.

Because \Sigma  u_k = 0 (by (6) with m = 1),

 \Sigma  L_k^4 = 12\rho ^2 + 4a^2U, where U := \Sigma _{k=1}^{12} u_k^2.(11)

Opposite beacons are antipodal: P_{k+6} = -P_k, hence u_{k+6} = -u_k and from (5)

 L_{k+6}^2 = \rho  + 2a u_k.(12)

Therefore for k = 1,\ldots ,6

 L_k^2 L_{k+6}^2 = (\rho  - 2a u_k)(\rho  + 2a u_k) = \rho ^2 - 4a^2u_k^2,

and

 \Sigma _{k=1}^{6} L_k^2 L_{k+6}^2 = 6\rho ^2 - 2a^2U. (13)

Combining (11) and (13) yields the corrected coefficient

 Q = \Sigma  L_k^4 - 2 \Sigma _{k=1}^{6} L_k^2 L_{k+6}^2  
   = (12\rho ^2 + 4a^2U) - 2(6\rho ^2 - 2a^2U) = 8a^2U. (14)

4. Evaluation of U = \Sigma  u_k^2.  
Write z = |z| e^{i\varphi }.  Then u_k = |z| cos(\varphi  - \theta _k).  
Because the twelve directions \theta _k are equally spaced, the average of cos^2 over them is \frac{1}{2}, whence

 U = \Sigma  u_k^2 = 12\cdot |z|^2 / 2 = 6|z|^2.(15)

(One may also confirm this with roots of unity:  
U = \frac{1}{4}(z^2 \Sigma  \zeta ^{-2(k-1)} + \bar z^2 \Sigma  \zeta ^{2(k-1)} + 2|z|^2 \Sigma  1) = 6|z|^2.)

Substituting (15) into (14) gives

 Q = 48 a^2 |z|^2 \Leftrightarrow  |z|^2 = Q /(48 a^2). (16)

5. Altitude formula (part (b)).  
From (10) we have \rho  = S/12.  By definition \rho  = a^2 + h^2 + |z|^2, so

 h^2 = \rho  - a^2 - |z|^2 = S/12 - a^2 - Q /(48 a^2),(17)

which is exactly the asserted expression.

6. The first two Fourier coefficients (part (c)).  
First coefficient:

 F_1 = \Sigma  \zeta ^{k-1} L_k^2  
     = \Sigma  \zeta ^{k-1}(\rho  - 2a u_k)  
     = -2a \Sigma  u_k \zeta ^{k-1}  (because \Sigma  \zeta ^{k-1}=0).(18)

Using (8) with r = 1 we obtain

 \Sigma  u_k \zeta ^{k-1} = \frac{1}{2} z \Sigma  \zeta ^{0} + \frac{1}{2} \bar z \Sigma  \zeta ^{2(k-1)} = 6z,(19)

since \Sigma  \zeta ^{0}=12 and \Sigma  \zeta ^{2(k-1)}=0.  Hence

 F_1 = -12a z \Leftrightarrow  z = -F_1 /(12 a). (20)

Second coefficient:

 F_2 = \Sigma  \zeta ^{2(k-1)} L_k^2  
    = -2a \Sigma  u_k \zeta ^{2(k-1)}.(21)

Insert the decomposition of u_k again:

 \Sigma  u_k \zeta ^{2(k-1)}  
  = \frac{1}{2} z \Sigma  \zeta ^{(2-1)(k-1)} + \frac{1}{2} \bar z \Sigma  \zeta ^{(2+1)(k-1)}  
  = \frac{1}{2} z \Sigma  \zeta ^{(k-1)} + \frac{1}{2} \bar z \Sigma  \zeta ^{3(k-1)} = 0, (22)

because both geometric sums vanish by (6).  Therefore

 F_2 = 0. (23)

Equations (20) and (16) complete part (c).  The identity F_2 = 0 furnishes an
immediate consistency test: any experimental data with F_2\neq 0 cannot come from
a single point in space.

Thus all three coordinates (x, y, h) are uniquely reconstructed from the twelve
ranges, and the corrected derivation is internally consistent.
